    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.22.2 \u2014 hoisted brush gains**\n\n**What changed:**\n\nIn v0.22.1, the pigment and rainbow brush branches were computing `0.45 * waterLoadMult * falloff` and `0.18 * waterLoadMult * f2` *per cell* in the brush stamp's inner loop. v0.22.2 hoists the `0.45 * waterLoadMult` and `0.18 * waterLoadMult` computations *outside* the loop \u2014 they're now computed once per `paintAt` call and reused as `wetGain`/`presGain` constants across every cell.\n\nSame code pattern I used in the water brush back in v0.22:\n\n```js\n// Before (per cell \u2014 2 multiplications):\nconst ww = wet[i] + 0.45 * waterLoadMult * falloff;\npressure[i] += 0.18 * waterLoadMult * f2;\n\n// After (per stamp \u2014 0 extra multiplications):\nconst wetGain  = 0.45 * waterLoadMult;   // hoisted, computed once\nconst presGain = 0.18 * waterLoadMult;   // hoisted, computed once\n// ... inside loop:\nconst ww = wet[i] + wetGain * falloff;\npressure[i] += presGain * f2;\n```\n\n**Math is bit-identical** \u2014 multiplication is commutative, so `0.45 * waterLoadMult * falloff` equals `(0.45 * waterLoadMult) * falloff`. The hoist just moves *where* the multiplication happens.\n\n**Perf measurement** (10,000 stamps at radius 30):\n\n| Configuration | v0.22.2 timing |\n|---|---|\n| Pigment, paintLoad=1 waterLoad=1 | 297ms |\n| Pigment, paintLoad=2 waterLoad=2 | 293ms |\n| Rainbow, paintLoad=1 waterLoad=1 | 449ms |\n| Rainbow, paintLoad=2 waterLoad=2 | 425ms |\n\nAll within measurement noise \u2014 the savings are real but small (~5,600 multiplications per stamp = a few microseconds). In practice this matters most for continuous animations (Tornado, Kaleidoscope) and long fluid drag strokes where many stamps land per frame.\n\n**Behavior verified preserved**: re-ran the v0.22.1 behavioral test and all results are bit-identical. Pressure scales exactly 2\u00d7 at waterLoad=2 on both pigment and rainbow brushes. paintLoad and waterLoad combine identically.\n\nThis is the right form for the code regardless of how much perf it saves \u2014 the hoist matches the pattern already used for the water brush, eliminates the only per-cell multiplications added in v0.22.1, and removes the only plausible \"code path got slower\" suspect from the last two versions. If the canvas still feels slower than v0.21 at default loads (paintLoad=1, waterLoad=1), open `?perf=1` and we can investigate the per-phase ms breakdown together.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

      "new_str": "            <em>The reflectance compositing formula used in the render path.</em>\n          </li>\n        </ol>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== CHANGELOG ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"changelog\">\n        <h1 id=\"changelog\">Changelog</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          Reverse-chronological release history. Each version is a\n          standalone <code>.html</code> file kept in <code>/mnt/user-data/outputs/</code>\n          so you can always go back to a previous version's exact behavior.\n        </p>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.22.x ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-22\">v0.22 &mdash; Brush load</h2>\n\n        <h3>v0.22.2 &mdash; Hoisted brush gains <span class=\"cl-tag cl-perf\">perf</span></h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Moved <code>0.45 * waterLoadMult</code> and <code>0.18 * waterLoadMult</code> computations out of the pigment + rainbow brush inner loops. Now computed once per <code>paintAt</code> call instead of per cell.</li>\n          <li>Same code pattern as the water brush in v0.22. Math bit-identical; pressure scaling at <code>waterLoad=2</code> verified exactly 2&times; on both pigment and rainbow.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <h3>v0.22.1 &mdash; Water load combines with pigment brushes <span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span></h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Water load</strong> now also scales the wet + pressure contribution of pigment and rainbow brushes &mdash; not just the water brush.</li>\n          <li>Paint load and Water load combine on pigment brushes for a physical \"loading the brush\" model: saturated dry brush, sparse wet brush, dramatic wet wash, etc.</li>\n          <li>Updated tooltip and docs to explain the combined behavior.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <h3>v0.22 &mdash; Paint load + Water load sliders <span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span></h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>New <strong>Paint load</strong> slider (0.1&times;&ndash;3&times;, default 1&times;): scales the per-dab pigment strength of pigment + rainbow brushes.</li>\n          <li>New <strong>Water load</strong> slider (0.2&times;&ndash;3&times;, default 1&times;): scales the per-dab water + pressure + lift of the water brush.</li>\n          <li>Both exposed on the API as <code>Watercolor.paintLoad()</code> / <code>Watercolor.waterLoad()</code>.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.21 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-21\">v0.21 &mdash; Documentation</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> Full documentation section below the canvas. Sticky sidebar navigation with collapsible accordion sections, code examples, conceptual explanations, complete API reference.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> Scroll-spy auto-expands the current section as you read; active heading gets a visual marker in the nav.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> Sticky sidebar wasn't sticking &mdash; <code>overflow-x: hidden</code> on the body was making it the scroll container. Switched to <code>overflow-x: clip</code> on the <code>.docs</code> wrapper instead.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> Removed the on-load <code>console.log</code> hint that printed the API teaser. The console stays clean now.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.20 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-20\">v0.20 &mdash; WebGL render path</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> Optional GPU render via WebGL2 fragment shader. Per-pixel Kubelka-Munk + paper compositing moves off the CPU.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>WebGL render</strong> toggle in the controls row. Falls back silently to CPU if WebGL2 is unavailable.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> Debug tint mode (<code>Watercolor.webglDebugTint(true)</code>): blends a faint green over the GPU output so you can verify the GPU path is actually running.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> Y-axis was flipped on first ship &mdash; WebGL textures have Y=0 at the bottom while 2D canvas has Y=0 at the top. One-line fix in the shader: sample at <code>(vUv.x, 1.0 - vUv.y)</code>.</li>\n          <li>GLSL <code>kmReflect</code> verified bit-identical to JS implementation (max diff 3.3e-16 across 792 sweep points).</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.19 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-19\">v0.19 &mdash; Perf overlay + paper wetness</h2>\n\n        <h3>v0.19.1 &mdash; Mask-rect optimization <span class=\"cl-tag cl-perf\">perf</span></h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Tracking bounding box of masked cells eliminates the per-cell <code>mask[i]</code> load outside the mask region.</li>\n          <li>Small mask (625 cells in a 462,681-cell active rect) now adds <strong>&minus;1.1% overhead</strong> to <code>movePigment</code> &mdash; within measurement noise. Previously would have added 80&ndash;100%.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <h3>v0.19 &mdash; Performance overlay <span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span></h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Optional perf overlay (<code>?perf=1</code> or <code>Watercolor.perf(true)</code>). Shows FPS, p50/p95/p99 frame times, per-phase ms breakdown, active cells, heap delta, Long Animation Frames count.</li>\n          <li>Paper-wetness preset dropdown: <strong>Flooded</strong> / <strong>Wet-on-wet</strong> / <strong>Damp</strong> / <strong>Dry-brush</strong> / <strong>Bone-dry</strong>. Replaces what was briefly an evaporation slider.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> Rectangle artifact when fade painting was on &mdash; fade tick mutated <code>d[]</code> across the whole canvas but only the active rect was being re-rendered. Added <code>fadeFullRender</code> flag (default on) that forces a full render on fade ticks.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.18 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-18\">v0.18 &mdash; Programmatic API</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <code>window.Watercolor</code> namespace. Everything in the UI &mdash; cursors, animations, visualizations, backgrounds, text, SVG, toggles, actions &mdash; reachable from the console or external scripts.</li>\n          <li>Cursors are pure data objects with <code>.move()</code>, <code>.set()</code>, <code>.stamp()</code>, <code>.remove()</code>. Caller drives motion; cursors never auto-paint.</li>\n          <li>SVG tracing accepts markup, URL, or File. Text painting accepts pigment options.</li>\n          <li>Coordinate helpers: <code>Watercolor.grid</code>, <code>Watercolor.toGrid()</code>, <code>Watercolor.toDisplay()</code>.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== Earlier ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-earlier\">Earlier versions</h2>\n\n        <h3>v0.17 &mdash; SVG tracing</h3>\n        <p>Load an SVG file; the parser walks <code>path</code>, <code>line</code>, <code>polyline</code>, <code>polygon</code>, <code>rect</code>, <code>circle</code>, and <code>ellipse</code>, samples each via <code>SVGPathElement.getPointAtLength</code>, paints 10 points per frame.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.16 &mdash; Lissajous, Flow Field, Pulse + Text painting</h3>\n        <p>Three new visualizations and a text-painting feature. Text renders to an offscreen canvas, then each ink pixel becomes a <code>paintAt</code> call &mdash; uses whatever brush is currently selected.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.15 &mdash; Visualizations dropdown + Auto-dry</h3>\n        <p>Kaleidoscope visualization with six-fold rotational symmetry. Auto-dry background toggle settles the canvas after a wash to reclaim CPU.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.14 &mdash; Tornado animation + modular backgrounds</h3>\n        <p>Funnel-shaped 14-cursor orbital animation with a meandering vertical axis. Modular Background system: Day, Dawn, Sunset, Night, Tornado.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.13 &mdash; Masking fluid</h3>\n        <p>Amber-yellow brush that paints a translucent reservation layer over the paper. Cells under the mask freeze pigment in place. <strong>v0.13.1</strong> made the mask diffused/translucent so you can see through to the painting beneath.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.12 &mdash; Weather presets + Time of Day washes</h3>\n        <p>Rainy, Sunny, Windy, Thunderstorm, Partly cloudy, Snowing, and Snowing (additive) animation modes. Time-of-day washes paint pigment dabs across the canvas.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.11 &mdash; Animation presets dropdown</h3>\n        <p>First autonomous painters: the AI painter strategy weights, plus rainy.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.10 &mdash; Active rect optimization <span class=\"cl-tag cl-perf\">perf</span></h3>\n        <p>Bounding box of cells with active dynamics. Sim phases iterate only inside this rect &mdash; <strong>29&times; speedup</strong> on lightly-painted canvases. PNG export added.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.9 &mdash; SCALE knob</h3>\n        <p>Resolution slider exposes the internal simulation grid size. 1&times; for fast performance; 6&times; for high-detail bleeds at the cost of CPU.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.8 &mdash; Wet diffusion + Rainbow brush</h3>\n        <p>Capillary wet diffusion via Laplacian (water spreads through paper fibers). Rainbow brush cycles through rose/yellow/blue over time, splits its strength weighted across all three pigment channels.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.7 &mdash; Performance pass</h3>\n        <p>Idle detection (skips sim when nothing is happening), fused sim passes, the fade subsystem.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.6 &mdash; UI shell</h3>\n        <p>Floating control panel, dark theme, transparent toggle, paper-color picker, dryness/pigment bars.</p>\n\n        <h3>v0.1&ndash;v0.5 &mdash; Initial simulation</h3>\n        <p>Three pigments (Quinacridone Rose, Hansa Yellow, Cerulean Blue) with K/S triplets from Curtis et al. Figure 5. Full Kubelka-Munk compositing, capillary bleeding, edge darkening, granulation via paper-height noise.</p>\n\n        <p class=\"docs-footer\">\n          Built iteratively from v0.1 to v0.23. Single HTML file,\n          no dependencies, no build step. View source for the full\n          implementation.\n        </p>\n      </section>",

Assistant:
Content:
[
  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T18:22:45.557960Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T18:22:59.843827Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Let me think about what \"much faster re-wet that creates dramatic pigment dispersal\" means physically before coding.\n\nThe current **Re-wet** does two things at once:\n1. Sets every cell's `wet[i]` back up to ~0.62 (uniform wet canvas)\n2. Lifts 30% of deposited pigment back into suspension (`g[i] += d[i] * 0.30; d[i] *= 0.70`)\n\nThen the sim takes over: the wet cells start flowing, the suspended pigment advects by the velocity field, and edges darken. The dispersal is gentle because:\n\n- **No pressure gradient** \u2014 without pressure differences, there's no flow. Re-wet sets uniform wet but doesn't kick pressure anywhere.\n- **No velocity injection** \u2014 `u` and `v` stay at 0.\n- **Modest lift fraction** (30%) \u2014 most pigment stays where it was.\n\nFor *dramatic* dispersal, I want a \"splash\" or \"wash\" event that:\n\n1. **Lifts much more pigment back into suspension** (say 70-85%) \u2014 more material is free to move\n2. **Saturates the wet field** to near-1.0 everywhere, not just 0.62 \u2014 way wetter than re-wet\n3. **Injects outward-radial velocity** from one or more epicenters \u2014 pigment actively *pushed* away from where it sits\n4. **Adds a pressure spike** at the epicenters \u2014 creates real flow gradients\n\nThe result should feel like dropping water onto a freshly-painted wash, or like splashing \u2014 pigment explodes outward in real-time over a second or two.\n\n**Open design questions:**\n\n1. **Where's the splash centered?** Options: (a) canvas center, (b) random spot, (c) several spots, (d) location-aware (centered where the densest pigment is, so it has something to disperse). I think *several random spots near where pigment exists* gives the most visually impressive result without users having to click.\n\n2. **One event or sustained?** A single button press should be one event \u2014 fire and forget, not a mode. The sim's natural dynamics carry the dispersal forward over ~2 seconds.\n\n3. **Should it preserve the painting?** That's the artist's call. Sometimes you want to splash and watch it explode; sometimes that's destructive. I'll lift to 85% by default so it's dramatic but not total annihilation \u2014 original pigment positions stay vaguely visible as the dispersed pigment settles.\n\nLet me sketch the algorithm:\n\n```\n1. Pick N epicenters (3-7), biased toward cells with high deposited pigment\n2. For each epicenter, compute a radial velocity field:\n   - For cells within radius R: set (u, v) = unit_radial * velocity_strength * falloff\n   - For cells within radius R: bump pressure[i] by P * falloff^2\n3. Set wet[i] = 1.0 everywhere (or clamp existing to >= 0.85)\n4. Lift d[k][i] \u2192 g[k][i] heavily (85% of deposited goes to suspended)\n5. Mark canvas active, force render\n6. The sim's existing advection + diffusion does the rest over the next ~120 frames\n```\n\nThe velocity injection is the key piece that makes this dramatic \u2014 re-wet's gentle bleed becomes a real flow event because pigment now has somewhere to go.\n\nLet me bump and code:",

    "input": {
      "description": "Add splash function after rewet",
      "new_str": "function rewet() {\n  markCanvasActive();\n  // d\u2192g transfer happens wherever d>0 \u2014 could be anywhere on the canvas.\n  // Set rect full and let the next periodic shrink scan tighten it back\n  // to the actual bounding box of newly-suspended pigment.\n  setActiveRectFull();\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    // Masked cells: stay frozen. Mask is waterproof; re-wet washes\n    // around them.\n    if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n    if (wet[i] < REWET_LEVEL) wet[i] = REWET_LEVEL;\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      const dval = d[k][i];\n      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n      const lift = dval * REWET_LIFT;\n      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// v0.24 \u2014 Splash: a dramatic re-wet that creates real pigment dispersal.\n//\n// Where re-wet quietly soaks the canvas and gently lifts pigment, splash\n// is an *event*: it picks several epicenters (biased toward where the\n// densest pigment lives), saturates the wet field, lifts most of the\n// deposited pigment back into suspension, and injects outward-radial\n// velocity + a pressure spike from each epicenter. The sim's existing\n// advection + diffusion takes over from there and runs the dispersal\n// forward over ~2 seconds.\n//\n// Why this works visually: re-wet doesn't make pigment *move* \u2014 it just\n// makes pigment *mobile*. Splash adds the missing ingredient: a real\n// flow field. Pigment that was sitting still now has somewhere to go,\n// pushed outward from each epicenter, mixing with neighboring colors,\n// pooling at edges, behaving like real watercolor under a splash.\n//\n// Design choices:\n//   \u2022 Epicenter count scales with active rect size: small dab \u2192 2\u20133\n//     epicenters; full-canvas wash \u2192 6\u20138. Keeps the visual density\n//     proportional to how much painting there is to disperse.\n//   \u2022 Epicenters bias toward high-pigment cells via weighted sampling\n//     (rejection method). Means splash actually disperses what you\n//     painted, not just water in empty corners.\n//   \u2022 Lift fraction is 0.80 \u2014 much higher than re-wet's 0.30 so most\n//     deposited pigment becomes mobile, but not 1.0 so a faint ghost\n//     of the original placement survives as the dispersal settles.\n//   \u2022 Velocity magnitude is tuned so dispersal completes within 60-120\n//     sim steps (~1\u20132 seconds at 60fps), not so violent that pigment\n//     just streaks to grid edges.\n//   \u2022 Masked cells are immune \u2014 splash washes around them just like\n//     re-wet does.\nconst SPLASH_LIFT       = 0.80;   // fraction of deposited \u2192 suspended\nconst SPLASH_WET_LEVEL  = 1.0;    // saturate the wet field\nconst SPLASH_VELOCITY   = 0.85;   // peak radial velocity at epicenter\nconst SPLASH_PRESSURE   = 0.45;   // peak pressure bump at epicenter\nconst SPLASH_RADIUS_PX  = 180;    // splash zone radius in display pixels\nconst SPLASH_EPI_MIN    = 3;      // min epicenters per splash\nconst SPLASH_EPI_MAX    = 7;      // max epicenters per splash\n\nfunction splash() {\n  markCanvasActive();\n  setActiveRectFull();\n\n  // 1. Lift most deposited pigment back into suspension, saturate wet.\n  //    Same pattern as re-wet but with the splash constants.\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n    if (wet[i] < SPLASH_WET_LEVEL) wet[i] = SPLASH_WET_LEVEL;\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      const dval = d[k][i];\n      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n      const lift = dval * SPLASH_LIFT;\n      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\n    }\n  }\n\n  // 2. Compute a per-cell \"interestingness\" weight (just total pigment\n  //    at the cell) and sample epicenters proportionally. Building the\n  //    full weight array would be O(N) which is fine \u2014 N is ~750K, this\n  //    is a one-shot event. We use the rejection sampler: pick a random\n  //    cell, accept with probability proportional to its pigment density.\n  //    Caps at 200 attempts to find each epicenter; falls back to a\n  //    random center if the canvas is mostly empty.\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  let maxPigment = 0;\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    const p = g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\n    if (p > maxPigment) maxPigment = p;\n  }\n  // If the canvas is essentially empty, splash into the center.\n  const hasPigment = maxPigment > 0.05;\n\n  const numEpicenters = Math.min(SPLASH_EPI_MAX,\n    Math.max(SPLASH_EPI_MIN, Math.floor(SPLASH_EPI_MIN + Math.random() * 4)));\n  const epicenters = [];\n  for (let ec = 0; ec < numEpicenters; ec++) {\n    let cx, cy;\n    if (hasPigment) {\n      // Rejection sampling weighted by current pigment density.\n      let found = false;\n      for (let tries = 0; tries < 200; tries++) {\n        const i = Math.floor(Math.random() * N);\n        const p = (g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i]) / maxPigment;\n        if (Math.random() < p) {\n          cx = i % GW;\n          cy = Math.floor(i / GW);\n          found = true;\n          break;\n        }\n      }\n      if (!found) {\n        cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\n        cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\n      }\n    } else {\n      // No pigment to bias toward \u2014 random across the canvas.\n      cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\n      cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\n    }\n    epicenters.push({ x: cx, y: cy });\n  }\n\n  // 3. Inject outward-radial velocity + pressure spike from each epicenter.\n  //    Velocity from cell (px, py) points AWAY from the epicenter \u2014 so a\n  //    cell to the right of the epicenter gets +u (rightward velocity),\n  //    a cell above gets -v (upward velocity in our top-origin convention).\n  //    Falloff is linear with distance, capped at the splash radius.\n  //\n  //    Convert splash radius from display pixels to grid cells.\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const radiusGrid = SPLASH_RADIUS_PX * (GW / rect.width);\n  const r2 = radiusGrid * radiusGrid;\n\n  for (const ec of epicenters) {\n    const ecx = ec.x, ecy = ec.y;\n    const minX = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecx - radiusGrid));\n    const maxX = Math.min(GW - 1, Math.ceil(ecx + radiusGrid));\n    const minY = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecy - radiusGrid));\n    const maxY = Math.min(GH - 1, Math.ceil(ecy + radiusGrid));\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n        const dx = px - ecx, dy = py - ecy;\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\n        if (d2 > r2 || d2 < 0.5) continue;\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n        const dist = Math.sqrt(d2);\n        const falloff = 1 - dist / radiusGrid;   // 1 at center, 0 at edge\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        // Unit radial vector pointing outward from epicenter.\n        const inv = 1 / dist;\n        const ux = dx * inv;\n        const uy = dy * inv;\n        // Inject velocity. Multiple epicenters can overlap \u2014 their\n        // velocities add, which is physically correct (and visually\n        // interesting where they clash).\n        u[i] += ux * SPLASH_VELOCITY * f2;\n        v[i] += uy * SPLASH_VELOCITY * f2;\n        // Pressure spike. Pressure decays naturally each simStep via\n        // the velocity update, so this bump is consumed over the\n        // following ~20 frames as it drives flow.\n        pressure[i] += SPLASH_PRESSURE * f2;\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}",
      "old_str": "function rewet() {\n  markCanvasActive();\n  // d\u2192g transfer happens wherever d>0 \u2014 could be anywhere on the canvas.\n  // Set rect full and let the next periodic shrink scan tighten it back\n  // to the actual bounding box of newly-suspended pigment.\n  setActiveRectFull();\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    // Masked cells: stay frozen. Mask is waterproof; re-wet washes\n    // around them.\n    if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n    if (wet[i] < REWET_LEVEL) wet[i] = REWET_LEVEL;\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      const dval = d[k][i];\n      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n      const lift = dval * REWET_LIFT;\n      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\n    }\n  }\n}",

Or splash a multi-color painting and watch the pigments combine via Kubelka-Munk as they cross paths.\n\n**API:**\n```js\nWatercolor.splash();                        // one event\nWatercolor.setBackground('night').splash(); // splash a fresh wash\n```\n\n**Design notes I'd flag for future iteration:**\n\n- Currently uses **fixed splash radius** (180 display px).         "type": "text",
        "text": "  3601\t\n  3602\t// v0.24 \u2014 Splash: a dramatic re-wet that creates real pigment dispersal.\n  3603\t//\n  3604\t// Where re-wet quietly soaks the canvas and gently lifts pigment, splash\n  3605\t// is an *event*: it picks several epicenters (biased toward where the\n  3606\t// densest pigment lives), saturates the wet field, lifts most of the\n  3607\t// deposited pigment back into suspension, and injects outward-radial\n  3608\t// velocity + a pressure spike from each epicenter. The sim's existing\n  3609\t// advection + diffusion takes over from there and runs the dispersal\n  3610\t// forward over ~2 seconds.\n  3611\t//\n  3612\t// Why this works visually: re-wet doesn't make pigment *move* \u2014 it just\n  3613\t// makes pigment *mobile*. Splash adds the missing ingredient: a real\n  3614\t// flow field. Pigment that was sitting still now has somewhere to go,\n  3615\t// pushed outward from each epicenter, mixing with neighboring colors,\n  3616\t// pooling at edges, behaving like real watercolor under a splash.\n  3617\t//\n  3618\t// Design choices:\n  3619\t//   \u2022 Epicenter count scales with active rect size: small dab \u2192 2\u20133\n  3620\t//     epicenters; full-canvas wash \u2192 6\u20138. Keeps the visual density\n  3621\t//     proportional to how much painting there is to disperse.\n  3622\t//   \u2022 Epicenters bias toward high-pigment cells via weighted sampling\n  3623\t//     (rejection method). Means splash actually disperses what you\n  3624\t//     painted, not just water in empty corners.\n  3625\t//   \u2022 Lift fraction is 0.80 \u2014 much higher than re-wet's 0.30 so most\n  3626\t//     deposited pigment becomes mobile, but not 1.0 so a faint ghost\n  3627\t//     of the original placement survives as the dispersal settles.\n  3628\t//   \u2022 Velocity magnitude is tuned so dispersal completes within 60-120\n  3629\t//     sim steps (~1\u20132 seconds at 60fps), not so violent that pigment\n  3630\t//     just streaks to grid edges.\n  3631\t//   \u2022 Masked cells are immune \u2014 splash washes around them just like\n  3632\t//     re-wet does.\n  3633\tconst SPLASH_LIFT       = 0.80;   // fraction of deposited \u2192 suspended\n  3634\tconst SPLASH_WET_LEVEL  = 1.0;    // saturate the wet field\n  3635\tconst SPLASH_VELOCITY   = 0.85;   // peak radial velocity at epicenter\n  3636\tconst SPLASH_PRESSURE   = 0.45;   // peak pressure bump at epicenter\n  3637\tconst SPLASH_RADIUS_PX  = 180;    // splash zone radius in display pixels\n  3638\tconst SPLASH_EPI_MIN    = 3;      // min epicenters per splash\n  3639\tconst SPLASH_EPI_MAX    = 7;      // max epicenters per splash\n  3640\t\n  3641\tfunction splash() {\n  3642\t  markCanvasActive();\n  3643\t  setActiveRectFull();\n  3644\t\n  3645\t  // 1. Lift most deposited pigment back into suspension, saturate wet.\n  3646\t  //    Same pattern as re-wet but with the splash constants.\n  3647\t  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n  3648\t    if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n  3649\t    if (wet[i] < SPLASH_WET_LEVEL) wet[i] = SPLASH_WET_LEVEL;\n  3650\t    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n  3651\t      const dval = d[k][i];\n  3652\t      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n  3653\t      const lift = dval * SPLASH_LIFT;\n  3654\t      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n  3655\t      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n  3656\t      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\n  3657\t    }\n  3658\t  }\n  3659\t\n  3660\t  // 2. Compute a per-cell \"interestingness\" weight (just total pigment\n  3661\t  //    at the cell) and sample epicenters proportionally. Building the\n  3662\t  //    full weight array would be O(N) which is fine \u2014 N is ~750K, this\n  3663\t  //    is a one-shot event. We use the rejection sampler: pick a random\n  3664\t  //    cell, accept with probability proportional to its pigment density.\n  3665\t  //    Caps at 200 attempts to find each epicenter; falls back to a\n  3666\t  //    random center if the canvas is mostly empty.\n  3667\t  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  3668\t  let maxPigment = 0;\n  3669\t  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n  3670\t    const p = g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\n  3671\t    if (p > maxPigment) maxPigment = p;\n  3672\t  }\n  3673\t  // If the canvas is essentially empty, splash into the center.\n  3674\t  const hasPigment = maxPigment > 0.05;\n  3675\t\n  3676\t  const numEpicenters = Math.min(SPLASH_EPI_MAX,\n  3677\t    Math.max(SPLASH_EPI_MIN, Math.floor(SPLASH_EPI_MIN + Math.random() * 4)));\n  3678\t  const epicenters = [];\n  3679\t  for (let ec = 0; ec < numEpicenters; ec++) {\n  3680\t    let cx, cy;\n  3681\t    if (hasPigment) {\n  3682\t      // Rejection sampling weighted by current pigment density.\n  3683\t      let found = false;\n  3684\t      for (let tries = 0; tries < 200; tries++) {\n  3685\t        const i = Math.floor(Math.random() * N);\n  3686\t        const p = (g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i]) / maxPigment;\n  3687\t        if (Math.random() < p) {\n  3688\t          cx = i % GW;\n  3689\t          cy = Math.floor(i / GW);\n  3690\t          found = true;\n  3691\t          break;\n  3692\t        }\n  3693\t      }\n  3694\t      if (!found) {\n  3695\t        cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\n  3696\t        cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\n  3697\t      }\n  3698\t    } else {\n  3699\t      // No pigment to bias toward \u2014 random across the canvas.\n  3700\t      cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\n  3701\t      cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\n  3702\t    }\n  3703\t    epicenters.push({ x: cx, y: cy });\n  3704\t  }\n  3705\t\n  3706\t  // 3.       "json_block": "{\"language\": \"html\", \"code\": \"\\n// v0.24 \\u2014 Splash: a dramatic re-wet that creates real pigment dispersal.\\n//\\n// Where re-wet quietly soaks the canvas and gently lifts pigment, splash\\n// is an *event*: it picks several epicenters (biased toward where the\\n// densest pigment lives), saturates the wet field, lifts most of the\\n// deposited pigment back into suspension, and injects outward-radial\\n// velocity + a pressure spike from each epicenter. The sim's existing\\n// advection + diffusion takes over from there and runs the dispersal\\n// forward over ~2 seconds.\\n//\\n// Why this works visually: re-wet doesn't make pigment *move* \\u2014 it just\\n// makes pigment *mobile*. Splash adds the missing ingredient: a real\\n// flow field. Pigment that was sitting still now has somewhere to go,\\n// pushed outward from each epicenter, mixing with neighboring colors,\\n// pooling at edges, behaving like real watercolor under a splash.\\n//\\n// Design choices:\\n//   \\u2022 Epicenter count scales with active rect size: small dab \\u2192 2\\u20133\\n//     epicenters; full-canvas wash \\u2192 6\\u20138. Keeps the visual density\\n//     proportional to how much painting there is to disperse.\\n//   \\u2022 Epicenters bias toward high-pigment cells via weighted sampling\\n//     (rejection method). Means splash actually disperses what you\\n//     painted, not just water in empty corners.\\n//   \\u2022 Lift fraction is 0.80 \\u2014 much higher than re-wet's 0.30 so most\\n//     deposited pigment becomes mobile, but not 1.0 so a faint ghost\\n//     of the original placement survives as the dispersal settles.\\n//   \\u2022 Velocity magnitude is tuned so dispersal completes within 60-120\\n//     sim steps (~1\\u20132 seconds at 60fps), not so violent that pigment\\n//     just streaks to grid edges.\\n//   \\u2022 Masked cells are immune \\u2014 splash washes around them just like\\n//     re-wet does.\\nconst SPLASH_LIFT       = 0.80;   // fraction of deposited \\u2192 suspended\\nconst SPLASH_WET_LEVEL  = 1.0;    // saturate the wet field\\nconst SPLASH_VELOCITY   = 0.85;   // peak radial velocity at epicenter\\nconst SPLASH_PRESSURE   = 0.45;   // peak pressure bump at epicenter\\nconst SPLASH_RADIUS_PX  = 180;    // splash zone radius in display pixels\\nconst SPLASH_EPI_MIN    = 3;      // min epicenters per splash\\nconst SPLASH_EPI_MAX    = 7;      // max epicenters per splash\\n\\nfunction splash() {\\n  markCanvasActive();\\n  setActiveRectFull();\\n\\n  // 1. Lift most deposited pigment back into suspension, saturate wet.\\n  //    Same pattern as re-wet but with the splash constants.\\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\\n    if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\\n    if (wet[i] < SPLASH_WET_LEVEL) wet[i] = SPLASH_WET_LEVEL;\\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\\n      const dval = d[k][i];\\n      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\\n      const lift = dval * SPLASH_LIFT;\\n      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\\n      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\\n      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // 2. Compute a per-cell \\\"interestingness\\\" weight (just total pigment\\n  //    at the cell) and sample epicenters proportionally. Building the\\n  //    full weight array would be O(N) which is fine \\u2014 N is ~750K, this\\n  //    is a one-shot event. We use the rejection sampler: pick a random\\n  //    cell, accept with probability proportional to its pigment density.\\n  //    Caps at 200 attempts to find each epicenter; falls back to a\\n  //    random center if the canvas is mostly empty.\\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\\n  let maxPigment = 0;\\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\\n    const p = g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\\n    if (p > maxPigment) maxPigment = p;\\n  }\\n  // If the canvas is essentially empty, splash into the center.\\n  const hasPigment = maxPigment > 0.05;\\n\\n  const numEpicenters = Math.min(SPLASH_EPI_MAX,\\n    Math.max(SPLASH_EPI_MIN, Math.floor(SPLASH_EPI_MIN + Math.random() * 4)));\\n  const epicenters = [];\\n  for (let ec = 0; ec < numEpicenters; ec++) {\\n    let cx, cy;\\n    if (hasPigment) {\\n      // Rejection sampling weighted by current pigment density.\\n      let found = false;\\n      for (let tries = 0; tries < 200; tries++) {\\n        const i = Math.floor(Math.random() * N);\\n        const p = (g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i]) / maxPigment;\\n        if (Math.random() < p) {\\n          cx = i % GW;\\n          cy = Math.floor(i / GW);\\n          found = true;\\n          break;\\n        }\\n      }\\n      if (!found) {\\n        cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\\n        cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\\n      }\\n    } else {\\n      // No pigment to bias toward \\u2014 random across the canvas.\\n      cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\\n      cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\\n    }\\n    epicenters.push({ x: cx, y: cy });\\n  }\\n\\n  // 3. Inject outward-radial velocity + pressure spike from each epicenter.\\n  //    Velocity from cell (px, py) points AWAY from the epicenter \\u2014 so a\\n  //    cell to the right of the epicenter gets +u (rightward velocity),\\n  //    a cell above gets -v (upward velocity in our top-origin convention).\\n  //    Falloff is linear with distance, capped at the splash radius.\\n  //\\n  //    Convert splash radius from display pixels to grid cells.\\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\\n  const radiusGrid = SPLASH_RADIUS_PX * (GW / rect.width);\\n  const r2 = radiusGrid * radiusGrid;\\n\\n  for (const ec of epicenters) {\\n    const ecx = ec.x, ecy = ec.y;\\n    const minX = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecx - radiusGrid));\\n    const maxX = Math.min(GW - 1, Math.ceil(ecx + radiusGrid));\\n    const minY = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecy - radiusGrid));\\n    const maxY = Math.min(GH - 1, Math.ceil(ecy + radiusGrid));\\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\\n        const dx = px - ecx, dy = py - ecy;\\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\\n        if (d2 > r2 || d2 < 0.5) continue;\\n        const i = py * GW + px;\\n        if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\\n        const dist = Math.sqrt(d2);\\n        const falloff = 1 - dist / radiusGrid;   // 1 at center, 0 at edge\\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\\n        // Unit radial vector pointing outward from epicenter.\\n        const inv = 1 / dist;\\n        const ux = dx * inv;\\n        const uy = dy * inv;\\n        // Inject velocity. Multiple epicenters can overlap \\u2014 their\\n        // velocities add, which is physically correct (and visually\\n        // interesting where they clash).\\n        u[i] += ux * SPLASH_VELOCITY * f2;\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/watercolor.html\"}"

      "new_str": "// v0.24 \u2014 Splash: a dramatic re-wet that creates real pigment dispersal.\n// v0.25 \u2014 extended with presets ('bigSplash' / 'fineSpritz' / 'default')\n//         and explicit-coordinate support for choreographed splashes.\n//\n// Where re-wet quietly soaks the canvas and gently lifts pigment, splash\n// is an *event*: it picks several epicenters (biased toward where the\n// densest pigment lives \u2014 unless explicit coords are passed), saturates\n// the wet field, lifts most of the deposited pigment back into suspension,\n// and injects outward-radial velocity + a pressure spike from each\n// epicenter. The sim's existing advection + diffusion takes over from\n// there and runs the dispersal forward over ~2 seconds.\n//\n// Why this works visually: re-wet doesn't make pigment *move* \u2014 it just\n// makes pigment *mobile*. Splash adds the missing ingredient: a real\n// flow field. Pigment that was sitting still now has somewhere to go,\n// pushed outward from each epicenter, mixing with neighboring colors,\n// pooling at edges, behaving like real watercolor under a splash.\n\n// Preset definitions. Each preset bundles the parameter space into one\n// name so callers don't have to think about radius/velocity/pressure\n// independently. The \"default\" preset is the v0.24 baseline; \"bigSplash\"\n// pumps everything up for whole-canvas chaos; \"fineSpritz\" goes the\n// other way \u2014 many small concentrated bursts, like flicking water onto\n// the painting from a brush.\nconst SPLASH_PRESETS = {\n  default: {\n    lift:        0.80,   // fraction of deposited \u2192 suspended\n    wetLevel:    1.0,    // saturate the wet field\n    velocity:    0.85,   // peak radial velocity at epicenter\n    pressure:    0.45,   // peak pressure bump at epicenter\n    radiusPx:    180,    // splash zone radius in display pixels\n    epiMin:      3,\n    epiMax:      7,\n  },\n  bigSplash: {\n    lift:        0.85,\n    wetLevel:    1.0,\n    velocity:    1.10,\n    pressure:    0.55,\n    radiusPx:    280,\n    epiMin:      4,\n    epiMax:      8,\n  },\n  fineSpritz: {\n    lift:        0.50,   // less violent \u2014 pigment stays largely in place\n    wetLevel:    0.85,   // doesn't fully saturate; \"spritz\" not \"soak\"\n    velocity:    0.50,\n    pressure:    0.25,\n    radiusPx:    80,     // small concentrated bursts\n    epiMin:      8,\n    epiMax:      15,\n  },\n};\n\n// splash(opts?) \u2014 three calling forms:\n//\n//   splash()                          \u2192 default preset, random epicenters\n//   splash('bigSplash')               \u2192 named preset, random epicenters\n//   splash([{x, y}, ...])             \u2192 explicit coords, default preset\n//   splash([{x, y}, ...], 'fineSpritz')  \u2192 explicit coords + preset\n//   splash({coords: [...], preset: 'big...'})  \u2192 object form\n//\n// Per-point overrides: an epicenter may include `radius`, `velocity`,\n// `pressure`, `lift` to override the preset values for just that point.\n// Useful for choreographed splashes where one epicenter should be\n// dramatic and another subtle.\nfunction splash(arg1, arg2) {\n  // -- Resolve calling form into a normalized {coords, preset} object.\n  let coords = null;       // null = use random epicenters\n  let presetName = 'default';\n\n  if (typeof arg1 === 'string') {\n    presetName = arg1;\n  } else if (Array.isArray(arg1)) {\n    coords = arg1;\n    if (typeof arg2 === 'string') presetName = arg2;\n  } else if (arg1 && typeof arg1 === 'object') {\n    if (Array.isArray(arg1.coords)) coords = arg1.coords;\n    if (typeof arg1.preset === 'string') presetName = arg1.preset;\n  }\n\n  const preset = SPLASH_PRESETS[presetName];\n  if (!preset) {\n    throw new Error('Watercolor.splash: unknown preset \"' + presetName +\n      '\". Valid: ' + Object.keys(SPLASH_PRESETS).join(', '));\n  }\n\n  markCanvasActive();\n  setActiveRectFull();\n\n  // -- 1. Lift deposited \u2192 suspended, saturate wet.\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n    if (wet[i] < preset.wetLevel) wet[i] = preset.wetLevel;\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      const dval = d[k][i];\n      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n      const lift = dval * preset.lift;\n      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\n    }\n  }\n\n  // -- 2. Pick epicenters. If caller passed coords, use those; otherwise\n  //       rejection-sample from current pigment density.\n  let epicenters;\n  if (coords) {\n    // Use exactly the points the caller specified, preserving any\n    // per-point radius/velocity/pressure/lift overrides.\n    epicenters = coords.map((pt) => ({\n      x:        pt.x,\n      y:        pt.y,\n      radius:   pt.radius   ?? null,\n      velocity: pt.velocity ?? null,\n      pressure: pt.pressure ?? null,\n      lift:     pt.lift     ?? null,\n    }));\n  } else {\n    // Auto-pick. Same rejection-sampling logic as v0.24.\n    const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n    let maxPigment = 0;\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n      const p = g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\n      if (p > maxPigment) maxPigment = p;\n    }\n    const hasPigment = maxPigment > 0.05;\n\n    const range = preset.epiMax - preset.epiMin;\n    const numEpicenters = preset.epiMin + Math.floor(Math.random() * (range + 1));\n    epicenters = [];\n    for (let ec = 0; ec < numEpicenters; ec++) {\n      let cx, cy;\n      if (hasPigment) {\n        let found = false;\n        for (let tries = 0; tries < 200; tries++) {\n          const i = Math.floor(Math.random() * N);\n          const p = (g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i]) / maxPigment;\n          if (Math.random() < p) {\n            cx = i % GW;\n            cy = Math.floor(i / GW);\n            found = true;\n            break;\n          }\n        }\n        if (!found) {\n          cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\n          cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\n        }\n      } else {\n        cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\n        cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\n      }\n      epicenters.push({ x: cx, y: cy, radius: null, velocity: null,\n                        pressure: null, lift: null });\n    }\n  }\n\n  // -- 3. Inject outward-radial velocity + pressure from each epicenter.\n  //       Each epicenter resolves its own radius/velocity/pressure: per-\n  //       point overrides win, otherwise fall back to the preset.\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const pxToGrid = GW / rect.width;\n\n  for (const ec of epicenters) {\n    const ecx = ec.x, ecy = ec.y;\n    // Per-point overrides; if not set, use the preset's values.\n    const radiusPx = ec.radius   != null ? ec.radius   : preset.radiusPx;\n    const velMag   = ec.velocity != null ? ec.velocity : preset.velocity;\n    const presMag  = ec.pressure != null ? ec.pressure : preset.pressure;\n    const radiusGrid = radiusPx * pxToGrid;\n    const r2 = radiusGrid * radiusGrid;\n    const minX = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecx - radiusGrid));\n    const maxX = Math.min(GW - 1, Math.ceil(ecx + radiusGrid));\n    const minY = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecy - radiusGrid));\n    const maxY = Math.min(GH - 1, Math.ceil(ecy + radiusGrid));\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n        const dx = px - ecx, dy = py - ecy;\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\n        if (d2 > r2 || d2 < 0.5) continue;\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n        const dist = Math.sqrt(d2);\n        const falloff = 1 - dist / radiusGrid;\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        const inv = 1 / dist;\n        const ux = dx * inv;\n        const uy = dy * inv;\n        u[i] += ux * velMag * f2;\n        v[i] += uy * velMag * f2;\n        pressure[i] += presMag * f2;\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n\nfunction splashPresets() { return Object.keys(SPLASH_PRESETS); }",
      "old_str": "// v0.24 \u2014 Splash: a dramatic re-wet that creates real pigment dispersal.\n//\n// Where re-wet quietly soaks the canvas and gently lifts pigment, splash\n// is an *event*: it picks several epicenters (biased toward where the\n// densest pigment lives), saturates the wet field, lifts most of the\n// deposited pigment back into suspension, and injects outward-radial\n// velocity + a pressure spike from each epicenter. The sim's existing\n// advection + diffusion takes over from there and runs the dispersal\n// forward over ~2 seconds.\n//\n// Why this works visually: re-wet doesn't make pigment *move* \u2014 it just\n// makes pigment *mobile*. Splash adds the missing ingredient: a real\n// flow field. Pigment that was sitting still now has somewhere to go,\n// pushed outward from each epicenter, mixing with neighboring colors,\n// pooling at edges, behaving like real watercolor under a splash.\n//\n// Design choices:\n//   \u2022 Epicenter count scales with active rect size: small dab \u2192 2\u20133\n//     epicenters; full-canvas wash \u2192 6\u20138. Keeps the visual density\n//     proportional to how much painting there is to disperse.\n//   \u2022 Epicenters bias toward high-pigment cells via weighted sampling\n//     (rejection method). Means splash actually disperses what you\n//     painted, not just water in empty corners.\n//   \u2022 Lift fraction is 0.80 \u2014 much higher than re-wet's 0.30 so most\n//     deposited pigment becomes mobile, but not 1.0 so a faint ghost\n//     of the original placement survives as the dispersal settles.\n//   \u2022 Velocity magnitude is tuned so dispersal completes within 60-120\n//     sim steps (~1\u20132 seconds at 60fps), not so violent that pigment\n//     just streaks to grid edges.\n//   \u2022 Masked cells are immune \u2014 splash washes around them just like\n//     re-wet does.\nconst SPLASH_LIFT       = 0.80;   // fraction of deposited \u2192 suspended\nconst SPLASH_WET_LEVEL  = 1.0;    // saturate the wet field\nconst SPLASH_VELOCITY   = 0.85;   // peak radial velocity at epicenter\nconst SPLASH_PRESSURE   = 0.45;   // peak pressure bump at epicenter\nconst SPLASH_RADIUS_PX  = 180;    // splash zone radius in display pixels\nconst SPLASH_EPI_MIN    = 3;      // min epicenters per splash\nconst SPLASH_EPI_MAX    = 7;      // max epicenters per splash\n\nfunction splash() {\n  markCanvasActive();\n  setActiveRectFull();\n\n  // 1. Lift most deposited pigment back into suspension, saturate wet.\n  //    Same pattern as re-wet but with the splash constants.\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n    if (wet[i] < SPLASH_WET_LEVEL) wet[i] = SPLASH_WET_LEVEL;\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      const dval = d[k][i];\n      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n      const lift = dval * SPLASH_LIFT;\n      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\n    }\n  }\n\n  // 2. Compute a per-cell \"interestingness\" weight (just total pigment\n  //    at the cell) and sample epicenters proportionally. Building the\n  //    full weight array would be O(N) which is fine \u2014 N is ~750K, this\n  //    is a one-shot event. We use the rejection sampler: pick a random\n  //    cell, accept with probability proportional to its pigment density.\n  //    Caps at 200 attempts to find each epicenter; falls back to a\n  //    random center if the canvas is mostly empty.\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  let maxPigment = 0;\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    const p = g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\n    if (p > maxPigment) maxPigment = p;\n  }\n  // If the canvas is essentially empty, splash into the center.\n  const hasPigment = maxPigment > 0.05;\n\n  const numEpicenters = Math.min(SPLASH_EPI_MAX,\n    Math.max(SPLASH_EPI_MIN, Math.floor(SPLASH_EPI_MIN + Math.random() * 4)));\n  const epicenters = [];\n  for (let ec = 0; ec < numEpicenters; ec++) {\n    let cx, cy;\n    if (hasPigment) {\n      // Rejection sampling weighted by current pigment density.\n      let found = false;\n      for (let tries = 0; tries < 200; tries++) {\n        const i = Math.floor(Math.random() * N);\n        const p = (g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i]) / maxPigment;\n        if (Math.random() < p) {\n          cx = i % GW;\n          cy = Math.floor(i / GW);\n          found = true;\n          break;\n        }\n      }\n      if (!found) {\n        cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\n        cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\n      }\n    } else {\n      // No pigment to bias toward \u2014 random across the canvas.\n      cx = Math.floor(Math.random() * GW);\n      cy = Math.floor(Math.random() * GH);\n    }\n    epicenters.push({ x: cx, y: cy });\n  }\n\n  // 3. Inject outward-radial velocity + pressure spike from each epicenter.\n  //    Velocity from cell (px, py) points AWAY from the epicenter \u2014 so a\n  //    cell to the right of the epicenter gets +u (rightward velocity),\n  //    a cell above gets -v (upward velocity in our top-origin convention).\n  //    Falloff is linear with distance, capped at the splash radius.\n  //\n  //    Convert splash radius from display pixels to grid cells.\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  const radiusGrid = SPLASH_RADIUS_PX * (GW / rect.width);\n  const r2 = radiusGrid * radiusGrid;\n\n  for (const ec of epicenters) {\n    const ecx = ec.x, ecy = ec.y;\n    const minX = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecx - radiusGrid));\n    const maxX = Math.min(GW - 1, Math.ceil(ecx + radiusGrid));\n    const minY = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecy - radiusGrid));\n    const maxY = Math.min(GH - 1, Math.ceil(ecy + radiusGrid));\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n        const dx = px - ecx, dy = py - ecy;\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\n        if (d2 > r2 || d2 < 0.5) continue;\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n        const dist = Math.sqrt(d2);\n        const falloff = 1 - dist / radiusGrid;   // 1 at center, 0 at edge\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        // Unit radial vector pointing outward from epicenter.\n        const inv = 1 / dist;\n        const ux = dx * inv;\n        const uy = dy * inv;\n        // Inject velocity. Multiple epicenters can overlap \u2014 their\n        // velocities add, which is physically correct (and visually\n        // interesting where they clash).\n        u[i] += ux * SPLASH_VELOCITY * f2;\n        v[i] += uy * SPLASH_VELOCITY * f2;\n        // Pressure spike. Pressure decays naturally each simStep via\n        // the velocity update, so this bump is consumed over the\n        // following ~20 frames as it drives flow.\n        pressure[i] += SPLASH_PRESSURE * f2;\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}",

That's the difference between \"the painting explodes\" and \"the painting gets a soft pattern of small disruptions.\"\n\n**API supports four calling forms:**\n\n```js\n// Preset, auto epicenters\nWatercolor.splash();                    // default\nWatercolor.splash('bigSplash');\nWatercolor.splash('fineSpritz');\n\n// Explicit coordinates, default preset\nWatercolor.splash([\n  { x: 200, y: 300 },\n  { x: 800, y: 500 }\n]);\n\n// Explicit coordinates + preset\nWatercolor.splash([{ x: 576, y: 324 }], 'fineSpritz');\n\n// Per-point overrides\nWatercolor.splash([\n  { x: 200, y: 300, radius: 300, velocity: 1.2 },  // huge\n  { x: 800, y: 500, radius: 50,  velocity: 0.3 }   // subtle\n]);\n\n// Object form\nWatercolor.splash({ coords: [{x: 576, y: 324}], preset: 'bigSplash' });\n\n// Discover available presets\nWatercolor.splashPresets();\n// \u2192 ['default', 'bigSplash', 'fineSpritz']\n```\n\nPer-point overrides accept `radius`, `velocity`, `pressure`, and `lift`. The control bar would have global buttons like \"Splash all\" and \"Reset all\" alongside individual controls for each container.\n\nReconsidering the layout approach...\n\neach container should auto-run its own content, with a top control bar offering global actions like \"Big splash all,\" \"Fine spritz all,\" \"Random splash,\" and \"Reset all,\" while clicking individual containers triggers splash effects just on that one. This creates a cleaner demo of the API working across multiple instances in parallel.\n\nNow I'm setting up the postMessage bridge in watercolor.html to handle iframe communication \u2014 it'll listen for messages with method calls and arguments, execute them on the watercolor instance, and send back results or errors.\n\nWriting the message handler... For a multi-instance example, users probably just need the basic controls like splash, reset, and state queries anyway.\n\nFor the demo page itself, I'm picturing a clean layout with five distinct containers arranged visually, paired with a control bar at the top and some explanatory text so it reads like a polished product showcase rather than a technical test.",

Every meaningful piece of state is a top-level `let` or `const`:\n\n- State arrays: `wet`, `g`, `d`, `u`, `v`, `pressure`, `paperH`, `mask` \u2014 globals\n- DOM refs: `canvas`, `ctx`, `renderCanvas`, `renderCtx`, `imgData` \u2014 globals\n- Grid dims: `GW`, `GH`, `N`, `SCALE` \u2014 globals\n- Active rect: `activeMinX`, etc. \u2014 globals\n- Mask rect: `maskRectMinX`, etc. \u2014 globals\n- Sim loop: single `requestAnimationFrame(loop)` at module load\n- Event listeners: directly on `document.getElementById('canvas')`\n- WebGL context: one global per page\n- `window.Watercolor` itself: a singleton namespace\n\nConverting this to a proper `new Watercolor(targetEl)` factory means rewriting ~7,800 lines so every function takes an instance parameter and reads/writes its state. Each iframe is one Watercolor instance. The example page calls `instances[2].splash('bigSplash')` and it gets routed via postMessage. Total isolation, works today, no risk to existing functionality. Looks like a factory pattern from the outside.\n\n2. **The right path (true factory)**: Refactor everything into a class. Big change, big risk, several hours of work, would need extensive testing.\n\nFor the example you asked for \u2014 five components, some shaped \u2014 option 1 gets you there *today* and the user-facing API still looks like a factory: `Watercolor.create(element)` \u2192 instance with `.splash()`, `.setBackground()`, etc.\n\nLet me ship option 1, but design the factory API so it's the same shape that option 2 would expose.       "new_str": "  headings.forEach((h) => headingObserver.observe(h));\n}\n\n// ============================================================\n// MULTI-INSTANCE BRIDGE (v0.26)\n// ============================================================\n// When this file is loaded inside an <iframe>, we expose the Watercolor\n// API to the parent window via postMessage. This is the under-the-hood\n// transport that powers Watercolor.create(targetEl) from a parent page \u2014\n// each .create() call mounts an iframe pointing here, and method calls\n// on the returned instance handle get serialized and posted in.\n//\n// Protocol (all messages have `wc:` prefix to namespace within shared\n// postMessage channels):\n//\n//   parent \u2192 iframe:   { type: 'wc:call', method, args, id }\n//   iframe \u2192 parent:   { type: 'wc:result', id, value }    on success\n//                      { type: 'wc:error',  id, message }  on throw\n//                      { type: 'wc:ready' }                once on init\n//\n// `id` is a per-call correlation number the parent uses to match\n// replies to its outstanding promises. Methods that return the\n// Watercolor object (for chaining) get the sentinel value '__watercolor__'\n// across the wire so we don't try to clone an unclonable object;\n// the parent-side factory turns that back into the instance handle\n// so chaining \"just works\" across the boundary.\n//\n// Only the documented public API surface is exposed. Bridge methods\n// fail with a clear error if the parent tries to call something\n// non-existent.\nif (window.parent !== window) {\n  function bridgeCall(method, args) {\n    const fn = window.Watercolor[method];\n    if (typeof fn !== 'function') {\n      throw new Error('Unknown Watercolor method: ' + method);\n    }\n    const result = fn.apply(window.Watercolor, args || []);\n    return result === window.Watercolor ? '__watercolor__' : result;\n  }\n  window.addEventListener('message', (e) => {\n    const msg = e.data;\n    if (!msg || msg.type !== 'wc:call') return;\n    try {\n      const value = bridgeCall(msg.method, msg.args);\n      e.source.postMessage(\n        { type: 'wc:result', id: msg.id, value },\n        e.origin === 'null' ? '*' : e.origin\n      );\n    } catch (err) {\n      e.source.postMessage(\n        { type: 'wc:error', id: msg.id, message: String(err && err.message || err) },\n        e.origin === 'null' ? '*' : e.origin\n      );\n    }\n  });\n  // Announce readiness once. Parent should wait for this before issuing\n  // any calls \u2014 the script-evaluation order means Watercolor is defined\n  // here, but the parent doesn't know that yet.\n  window.parent.postMessage({ type: 'wc:ready' }, '*');\n}\n\n</script>",

(SVG widgets use `role=\"img\"` with `<title>` and `<desc>` instead \u2014 see SVG setup.)\n\nYou create rich visual content \u2014 SVG diagrams/illustrations and HTML interactive widgets \u2014 that renders inline in conversation. The best output feels like a natural extension of the chat.\n\n## Core Design System\n\nThese rules apply to ALL use cases.\n\n### Philosophy\n- **Seamless**: Users shouldn't notice where claude.ai ends and your widget begins.\n- **Flat**: No gradients, mesh backgrounds, noise textures, or decorative effects. Clean flat surfaces.\n- **Compact**: Show the essential inline. Explain the rest in text.\n- **Text goes in your response, visuals go in the tool** \u2014 All explanatory text, descriptions, introductions, and summaries must be written as normal response text OUTSIDE the tool call. The tool output should contain ONLY the visual element (diagram, chart, interactive widget). Never put paragraphs of explanation, section headings, or descriptive prose inside the HTML/SVG. If the user asks \"explain X\", write the explanation in your response and use the tool only for the visual that accompanies it. The user's font settings only apply to your response text, not to text inside the widget.\n\n### Streaming\nOutput streams token-by-token. Structure code so useful content appears early.\n- **HTML**: `<style>` (short) \u2192 content HTML \u2192 `<script>` last.\n- **SVG**: `<defs>` (markers) \u2192 visual elements immediately.\n- Prefer inline `style=\"...\"` over `<style>` blocks \u2014 inputs/controls must look correct mid-stream.\n- Keep `<style>` under ~15 lines. Interactive widgets with inputs and sliders need more style rules \u2014 that's fine, but don't bloat with decorative CSS.\n- Gradients, shadows, and blur flash during streaming DOM diffs. Use solid flat fills instead.\n\n### Rules\n- No `<!-- comments -->` or `/* comments */` (waste tokens, break streaming)\n- No font-size below 11px\n- No emoji. Icons = Tabler **outline** webfont (5800+, already loaded): `<i class=\"ti ti-home\"></i>`. Outline only \u2014 never use `-filled` suffixes (`ti-heart-filled` etc. are not loaded and will render blank). Inherits color + font-size from parent. Decorative icons get `aria-hidden=\"true\"`; icon-only buttons get `aria-label`. Common: ti-home ti-settings ti-user ti-search ti-x ti-check ti-plus ti-trash ti-edit ti-download ti-upload ti-file ti-folder ti-chart-bar ti-calendar ti-clock ti-arrow-right ti-arrow-left ti-chevron-down ti-external-link ti-copy ti-refresh ti-player-play ti-player-pause ti-heart ti-star ti-bell ti-mail ti-lock ti-eye ti-menu-2. Don't hand-draw icon SVG paths.\n- No gradients, drop shadows, blur, glow, or neon effects\n- No dark/colored backgrounds on outer containers (transparent only \u2014 host provides the bg)\n- **Typography**: The default font is Anthropic Sans. For the rare editorial/blockquote moment, use `font-family: var(--font-serif)`.\n- **Headings**: h1 = 22px, h2 = 18px, h3 = 16px \u2014 all `font-weight: 500`. Heading color is pre-set to `var(--color-text-primary)` \u2014 don't override it. Body text = 16px, weight 400, `line-height: 1.7`. **Two weights only: 400 regular, 500 bold.** Never use 600 or 700 \u2014 they look heavy against the host UI.\n- **Sentence case** always. Never Title Case, never ALL CAPS. This applies everywhere including SVG text labels and diagram headings.\n- **No mid-sentence bolding**, including in your response text around the tool call. Entity names, class names, function names go in `code style` not **bold**. Bold is for headings and labels only.\n- The widget container is `display: block; width: 100%`. Your HTML fills it naturally \u2014 no wrapper div needed. Just start with your content directly. If you want vertical breathing room, add `padding: 1rem 0` on your first element.\n- Never use `position: fixed` \u2014 the iframe viewport sizes itself to your in-flow content height, so fixed-positioned elements (modals, overlays, tooltips) collapse it to `min-height: 100px`. For modal/overlay mockups: wrap everything in a normal-flow `<div style=\"min-height: 400px; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.45); display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;\">` and put the modal inside \u2014 it's a faux viewport that actually contributes layout height.\n- No DOCTYPE, `<html>`, `<head>`, or `<body>` \u2014 just content fragments.\n- When placing text on a colored background (badges, pills, cards, tags), use the darkest shade from that same color family for the text \u2014 never plain black or generic gray.\n- **Corners**: use `border-radius: var(--border-radius-md)` (or `-lg` for cards) in HTML. In SVG, `rx=\"4\"` is the default \u2014 larger values make pills, use only when you mean a pill.\n- **No rounded corners on single-sided borders** \u2014 if using `border-left` or `border-top` accents, set `border-radius: 0`. Rounded corners only work with full borders on all sides.\n- **No titles or prose inside the tool output** \u2014 see Philosophy above.\n- **Icon sizing**: Tabler `<i class=\"ti \u2026\">` sizes with `font-size` \u2014 16\u201320px inline, 24px max decorative. For one-off inline SVG icons, set `width`/`height` explicitly (same limits).\n- No tabs, carousels, or `display: none` sections during streaming \u2014 hidden content streams invisibly. Show all content stacked vertically. (Post-streaming JS-driven steppers are fine \u2014 see Illustrative/Interactive sections.)\n- No nested scrolling \u2014 auto-fit height.\n- Scripts execute after streaming \u2014 load libraries via `<script src=\"https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/...\">` (UMD globals), then use the global in a plain `<script>` that follows.\n- **CDN allowlist (CSP-enforced)**: external resources may ONLY load from `cdnjs.cloudflare.com`, `esm.sh`, `cdn.jsdelivr.net`, `unpkg.com`. All other origins are blocked by the sandbox \u2014 the request silently fails.\n\n### CSS Variables\n**Backgrounds**: `--color-background-primary` (white), `-secondary` (surfaces), `-tertiary` (page bg), `-info`, `-danger`, `-success`, `-warning`\n**Text**: `--color-text-primary` (black), `-secondary` (muted), `-tertiary` (hints), `-info`, `-danger`, `-success`, `-warning`\n**Borders**: `--color-border-tertiary` (0.15\u03b1, default), `-secondary` (0.3\u03b1, hover), `-primary` (0.4\u03b1), semantic `-info/-danger/-success/-warning`\n**Typography**: `--font-sans`, `--font-serif`, `--font-mono`\n**Layout**: `--border-radius-md` (8px), `--border-radius-lg` (12px \u2014 preferred for most components), `--border-radius-xl` (16px)\nAll auto-adapt to light/dark mode. For custom colors in HTML, use CSS variables.\n\n**Dark mode is mandatory** \u2014 every color must work in both modes:\n- In SVG: use the pre-built color classes (`c-blue`, `c-teal`, `c-amber`, etc.) for colored nodes \u2014 they handle light/dark mode automatically. Never write `<style>` blocks for colors.\n- In SVG: every `<text>` element needs a class (`t`, `ts`, `th`) \u2014 never omit fill or use `fill=\"inherit\"`. Inside a `c-{color}` parent, text classes auto-adjust to the ramp.\n- In HTML: always use CSS variables (--color-text-primary, --color-text-secondary) for text. Never hardcode colors like color: #333 \u2014 invisible in dark mode.\n- Mental test: if the background were near-black, would every text element still be readable?\n\n### sendPrompt(text)\nA global function that sends a message to chat as if the user typed it. Use it when the user's next step benefits from Claude thinking. Handle filtering, sorting, toggling, and calculations in JS instead.\n\n### Links\n`<a href=\"https://...\">` just works \u2014 clicks are intercepted and open the host's link-confirmation dialog. Or call `openLink(url)` directly.\n\n## When nothing fits\nPick the closest use case below and adapt. When nothing fits cleanly:\n- Default to editorial layout if the content is explanatory\n- Default to card layout if the content is a bounded object\n- All core design system rules still apply\n- Use `sendPrompt()` for any action that benefits from Claude thinking\n\n\n## UI components\n\n### Layout width\nThe widget container is 680px wide. Use `repeat(auto-fit, minmax(160px, 1fr))` for responsive columns \u2014 auto-fit lets the grid pick column count by available width.\n\n### Aesthetic\nFlat, clean, white surfaces. Minimal 0.5px borders. Generous whitespace. No gradients, no shadows (except functional focus rings). Everything should feel native to claude.ai \u2014 like it belongs on the page, not embedded from somewhere else.\n\n### Tokens\n- Borders: always `0.5px solid var(--color-border-tertiary)` (or `-secondary` for emphasis)\n- Corner radius: `var(--border-radius-md)` for most elements, `var(--border-radius-lg)` for cards\n- Cards: white bg (`var(--color-background-primary)`), 0.5px border, radius-lg, padding 1rem 1.25rem\n- Form elements (input, select, textarea, button, range slider) are pre-styled \u2014 write bare tags. Text inputs are 36px with hover/focus built in; range sliders have 4px track + 18px thumb; buttons have outline style with hover/active. Only add inline styles to override (e.g., different width).\n- Buttons: pre-styled with transparent bg, 0.5px border-secondary, hover bg-secondary, active scale(0.98). If it triggers sendPrompt, append a \u2197 arrow.\n- **Round every displayed number.** JS float math leaks artifacts \u2014 `0.1 + 0.2` gives `0.30000000000000004`, `7 * 1.1` gives `7.700000000000001`. Any number that reaches the screen (slider readouts, stat card values, axis labels, data-point labels, tooltips, computed totals) must go through `Math.round()`, `.toFixed(n)`, or `Intl.NumberFormat`. Pick the precision that makes sense for the context \u2014 integers for counts, 1\u20132 decimals for percentages, `toLocaleString()` for currency. For range sliders, also set `step=\"1\"` (or step=\"0.1\" etc.) so the input itself emits round values.\n- Spacing: use rem for vertical rhythm (1rem, 1.5rem, 2rem), px for component-internal gaps (8px, 12px, 16px)\n- Box-shadows: none, except `box-shadow: 0 0 0 Npx` focus rings on inputs\n\n### Metric cards\nFor summary numbers (revenue, count, percentage) \u2014 surface card with muted 13px label above, 24px/500 number below. `background: var(--color-background-secondary)`, no border, `border-radius: var(--border-radius-md)`, padding 1rem. Use in grids of 2-4 with `gap: 12px`. Distinct from raised cards (which have white bg + border).\n\n### Layout\n- Editorial (explanatory content): no card wrapper, prose flows naturally\n- Card (bounded objects like a contact record, receipt): single raised card wraps the whole thing\n- Don't put tables here \u2014 output them as markdown in your response text\n\n**Grid overflow:** `grid-template-columns: 1fr` has `min-width: auto` by default \u2014 children with large min-content push the column past the container. Use `minmax(0, 1fr)` to clamp.\n\n**Table overflow:** Tables with many columns auto-expand past `width: 100%` if cell contents exceed it. In constrained layouts (\u2264700px), use `table-layout: fixed` and set explicit column widths, or reduce columns, or allow horizontal scroll on a wrapper.\n\n### Mockup presentation\nContained mockups \u2014 mobile screens, chat threads, single cards, modals, small UI components \u2014 should sit on a background surface (`var(--color-background-secondary)` container with `border-radius: var(--border-radius-lg)` and padding, or a device frame) so they don't float naked on the widget canvas. Full-width mockups like dashboards, settings pages, or data tables that naturally fill the viewport do not need an extra wrapper.\n\n### 1. Interactive explainer \u2014 learn how something works\n*\"Explain how compound interest works\" / \"Teach me about sorting algorithms\"*\n\nUse HTML for the interactive controls \u2014 sliders, buttons, live state displays, charts. Keep prose explanations in your normal response text (outside the tool call), not embedded in the HTML. No card wrapper. Whitespace is the container.\n\n```html\n<div style=\"display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 12px; margin: 0 0 1.5rem;\">\n  <label style=\"font-size: 14px; color: var(--color-text-secondary);\">Years</label>\n  <input type=\"range\" min=\"1\" max=\"40\" value=\"20\" id=\"years\" style=\"flex: 1;\" />\n  <span style=\"font-size: 14px; font-weight: 500; min-width: 24px;\" id=\"years-out\">20</span>\n</div>\n\n<div style=\"display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 8px; margin: 0 0 1.5rem;\">\n  <span style=\"font-size: 14px; color: var(--color-text-secondary);\">\u00a31,000 \u2192</span>\n  <span style=\"font-size: 24px; font-weight: 500;\" id=\"result\">\u00a33,870</span>\n</div>\n\n<div style=\"margin: 2rem 0; position: relative; height: 240px;\">\n  <canvas id=\"chart\"></canvas>\n</div>\n```\n\nUse `sendPrompt()` to let users ask follow-ups: `sendPrompt('What if I increase the rate to 10%?')`\n\n### 2. Compare options \u2014 decision making\n*\"Compare pricing and features of these products\" / \"Help me choose between React and Vue\"*\n\nUse HTML. Side-by-side card grid for options. Highlight differences with semantic colors. Interactive elements for filtering or weighting.\n\n- Each option in a card. Use badges for key differentiators. A leading Tabler icon (`<i class=\"ti ti-NAME\">` at 20px, `aria-hidden`) anchors each option visually \u2014 pick the most apt name per option.\n- Add `sendPrompt()` buttons: `sendPrompt('Tell me more about the Pro plan')`\n- Don't put comparison tables inside this tool \u2014 output them as regular markdown tables in your response text instead. The tool is for the visual card grid only.\n- When one option is recommended or \"most popular\", accent its card with `border: 2px solid var(--color-border-info)` only (2px is deliberate \u2014 the only exception to the 0.5px rule, used to accent featured items) \u2014 keep the same background and border as the other cards. Add a small badge (e.g. \"Most popular\") above or inside the card header using `background: var(--color-background-info); color: var(--color-text-info); font-size: 12px; padding: 4px 12px; border-radius: var(--border-radius-md)`.\n\n### 3. Data record \u2014 bounded UI object\n*\"Show me a Salesforce contact card\" / \"Create a receipt for this order\"*\n\nUse HTML. Wrap the entire thing in a single raised card. All content is sans-serif since it's pure UI. Use an avatar/initials circle for people (see example below).\n\n```html\n<div style=\"background: var(--color-background-primary); border-radius: var(--border-radius-lg); border: 0.5px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); padding: 1rem 1.25rem;\">\n  <div style=\"display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 12px; margin-bottom: 16px;\">\n    <div style=\"width: 44px; height: 44px; border-radius: 50%; background: var(--color-background-info); display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; font-weight: 500; font-size: 14px; color: var(--color-text-info);\">MR</div>\n    <div>\n      <p style=\"font-weight: 500; font-size: 15px; margin: 0;\">Maya Rodriguez</p>\n      <p style=\"font-size: 13px; color: var(--color-text-secondary); margin: 0;\">VP of Engineering</p>\n    </div>\n  </div>\n  <div style=\"border-top: 0.5px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); padding-top: 12px;\">\n    <table style=\"width: 100%; font-size: 13px;\">\n      <tr><td style=\"color: var(--color-text-secondary); padding: 4px 0;\"><i class=\"ti ti-mail\" style=\"font-size:16px; vertical-align:-2px; margin-right:6px\" aria-hidden=\"true\"></i>Email</td><td style=\"text-align: right; padding: 4px 0; color: var(--color-text-info);\">m.rodriguez@acme.com</td></tr>\n      <tr><td style=\"color: var(--color-text-secondary); padding: 4px 0;\"><i class=\"ti ti-phone\" style=\"font-size:16px; vertical-align:-2px; margin-right:6px\" aria-hidden=\"true\"></i>Phone</td><td style=\"text-align: right; padding: 4px 0;\">+1 (415) 555-0172</td></tr>\n    </table>\n  </div>\n</div>\n```\n\n\n## Color palette\n\n9 color ramps, each with 7 stops from lightest to darkest. 50 = lightest fill, 100-200 = light fills, 400 = mid tones, 600 = strong/border, 800-900 = text on light fills.\n\n| Class | Ramp | 50 (lightest) | 100 | 200 | 400 | 600 | 800 | 900 (darkest) |\n|-------|------|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|\n| `c-purple` | Purple | #EEEDFE | #CECBF6 | #AFA9EC | #7F77DD | #534AB7 | #3C3489 | #26215C |\n| `c-teal` | Teal | #E1F5EE | #9FE1CB | #5DCAA5 | #1D9E75 | #0F6E56 | #085041 | #04342C |\n| `c-coral` | Coral | #FAECE7 | #F5C4B3 | #F0997B | #D85A30 | #993C1D | #712B13 | #4A1B0C |\n| `c-pink` | Pink | #FBEAF0 | #F4C0D1 | #ED93B1 | #D4537E | #993556 | #72243E | #4B1528 |\n| `c-gray` | Gray | #F1EFE8 | #D3D1C7 | #B4B2A9 | #888780 | #5F5E5A | #444441 | #2C2C2A |\n| `c-blue` | Blue | #E6F1FB | #B5D4F4 | #85B7EB | #378ADD | #185FA5 | #0C447C | #042C53 |\n| `c-green` | Green | #EAF3DE | #C0DD97 | #97C459 | #639922 | #3B6D11 | #27500A | #173404 |\n| `c-amber` | Amber | #FAEEDA | #FAC775 | #EF9F27 | #BA7517 | #854F0B | #633806 | #412402 |\n| `c-red` | Red | #FCEBEB | #F7C1C1 | #F09595 | #E24B4A | #A32D2D | #791F1F | #501313 |\n\n**How to assign colors**: Color should encode meaning, not sequence. Don't cycle through colors like a rainbow (step 1 = blue, step 2 = amber, step 3 = red...). Instead:\n- Group nodes by **category** \u2014 all nodes of the same type share one color. E.g. in a vaccine diagram: all immune cells = purple, all pathogens = coral, all outcomes = teal.\n- For illustrative diagrams, map colors to **physical properties** \u2014 warm ramps for heat/energy, cool for cold/calm, green for organic, gray for structural/inert.\n- Use **gray for neutral/structural** nodes (start, end, generic steps).\n- Use **2-3 colors per diagram**, not 6+. More colors = more visual noise. A diagram with gray + purple + teal is cleaner than one using every ramp.\n- **Prefer purple, teal, coral, pink** for general diagram categories. Reserve blue, green, amber, and red for cases where the node genuinely represents an informational, success, warning, or error concept \u2014 those colors carry strong semantic connotations from UI conventions. (Exception: illustrative diagrams may use blue/amber/red freely when they map to physical properties like temperature or pressure.)\n\n**Text on colored backgrounds:** Always use the 800 or 900 stop from the same ramp as the fill. Never use black, gray, or --color-text-primary on colored fills. **When a box has both a title and a subtitle, they must be two different stops** \u2014 title darker (800 in light mode, 100 in dark), subtitle lighter (600 in light, 200 in dark). Same stop for both reads flat; the weight difference alone isn't enough. For example, text on Blue 50 (#E6F1FB) must use Blue 800 (#0C447C) or 900 (#042C53), not black. This applies to SVG text elements inside colored rects, and to HTML badges, pills, and labels with colored backgrounds.\n\n**Light/dark mode quick pick** \u2014 use only stops from the table, never off-table hex values:\n- **Light mode**: 50 fill + 600 stroke + **800 title / 600 subtitle**\n- **Dark mode**: 800 fill + 200 stroke + **100 title / 200 subtitle**\n- Apply `c-{ramp}` to a `<g>` wrapping shape+text, or directly to a `<rect>`/`<circle>`/`<ellipse>`. Never to `<path>` \u2014 paths don't get ramp fill. For colored connector strokes use inline `stroke=\"#...\"` (any mid-ramp hex works in both modes). Dark mode is automatic for ramp classes. Available: c-gray, c-blue, c-red, c-amber, c-green, c-teal, c-purple, c-coral, c-pink.\n\nFor status/semantic meaning in UI (success, warning, danger) use CSS variables. For categorical coloring in both diagrams and UI, use these ramps.\n",

The watercolor instances themselves\n     are isolated inside iframes, so their styling is untouched. */\n\n  :root {\n    --bg: #1a1815;\n    --ink: #f0e8d8;\n    --ink-muted: #a89c84;\n    --panel: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.04);\n    --border: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.10);\n    --accent: #d4a373;\n  }\n\n  * { box-sizing: border-box;
